hook.text: An exoplanet is any planet that orbits a star other than the Sun. Over 5,600 have been confirmed so far.

buildup.text: For centuries, scientists suspected other stars had planets, but proof was impossible with old telescopes.

discovery.text: The first confirmed exoplanets were found in 1992—orbiting a pulsar, not a normal star. Nobody expected that.

twist.text: The first exoplanet around a Sun-like star came in 1995. 51 Pegasi b was a giant planet orbiting absurdly close to its star.

climax.text: Now we know planets are common. Statistically, every star in the Milky Way hosts at least one planet.

punchline.text: Five thousand worlds and counting. Planets are the rule, not the exception.

quiz.question: Where were the first confirmed exoplanets found?
options: Orbiting a pulsar, not a Sun-like star
Orbiting the nearest star to the Sun, Proxima Centauri
Floating freely in space with no star at all

hook.text: When a planet crosses in front of its star, the star's light dips slightly. That tiny dip reveals the planet.

buildup.text: The transit method measures how much light is blocked and for how long. This tells us the planet's size and orbit.

discovery.text: NASA's Kepler mission used this method to find over 2,600 exoplanets by staring at 150,000 stars for years.

twist.text: The dip is incredibly small—about 0.01% for an Earth-sized planet. It takes extraordinary precision to detect it.

climax.text: TESS, Kepler's successor, is surveying the entire sky. It's already found thousands more planet candidates.

punchline.text: A tiny shadow crossing a distant star. That's how we find new worlds.

quiz.question: How does the transit method detect exoplanets?
options: By measuring tiny dips in starlight when a planet passes in front of the star
By detecting gravitational waves from orbiting planets
By photographing planets directly next to their host stars

hook.text: A planet's gravity tugs on its star, making the star wobble. That wobble shifts the star's light in a detectable way.

buildup.text: As the star moves toward us, its light shifts blue. As it moves away, it shifts red. This is the Doppler effect.

discovery.text: Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz used this method to find 51 Pegasi b in 1995—earning them a Nobel Prize in 2019.

twist.text: This method reveals a planet's mass and orbit but not its size. It's complementary to the transit method.

climax.text: Modern spectrographs can detect star wobbles of just 1 m/s—a slow walking speed from light-years away.

punchline.text: Stars wobble when planets pull. That wobble crosses the galaxy to reach our instruments.

quiz.question: What does the radial velocity method measure to find exoplanets?
options: The wobble of a star caused by a planet's gravitational pull
The speed at which a planet orbits its star
Changes in a star's brightness over time

hook.text: Taking a photo of an exoplanet is incredibly hard. Stars are billions of times brighter than their planets.

buildup.text: Coronagraphs block the star's light, revealing faint planets nearby. It's like spotting a firefly next to a searchlight.

discovery.text: The first direct image came in 2004—a planet orbiting the brown dwarf 2M1207. It was a historic achievement.

twist.text: Direct imaging works best for young, hot, massive planets far from their stars. Small rocky worlds remain invisible.

climax.text: JWST's coronagraph and future missions like HWO aim to directly photograph Earth-like planets for the first time.

punchline.text: A firefly beside a searchlight. We're learning to see it clearly.

quiz.question: Why is direct imaging of exoplanets so difficult?
options: Stars are billions of times brighter than their planets, overwhelming the signal
Exoplanets move too quickly to be captured by cameras
The distances involved are too great for light to reach us

hook.text: When a star with a planet passes in front of a background star, gravity bends the light and briefly magnifies it.

buildup.text: The planet creates an extra spike in the magnification curve. This method can find planets thousands of light-years away.

discovery.text: OGLE-2005-BLG-390Lb was found this way—a cold, distant world 21,000 light-years away, one of the farthest known.

twist.text: Microlensing events are one-time—they never repeat. You get one chance to observe each detection.

climax.text: NASA's Roman Space Telescope will use microlensing to detect thousands of exoplanets, including free-floating ones.

punchline.text: Gravity bends light. One brief flash reveals a hidden world. No second chances.

quiz.question: What is unique about gravitational microlensing detections?
options: They are one-time events that never repeat
They can only detect gas giant planets
They require the planet to transit its star

hook.text: Hot Jupiters are gas giants that orbit insanely close to their stars—some completing a year in less than two days.

buildup.text: Their surface temperatures can reach 2,000°C. Some are so hot that iron and glass rain down from their clouds.

discovery.text: 51 Pegasi b was the first found—a Jupiter-mass planet orbiting its star in just 4.2 days. It shocked astronomers.

twist.text: They can't form where they are now. They must have migrated inward from farther out, rewriting planetary formation theory.

climax.text: Some are tidally locked—one side always faces the star. The day side roasts while the night side is far cooler.

punchline.text: Jupiter-sized, furnace-hot, impossibly close. Worlds that shouldn't be where they are.

quiz.question: Why do hot Jupiters challenge planetary formation theory?
options: They can't form so close to a star and must have migrated inward
They are too massive to have formed from a protoplanetary disk
Their atmospheres contain elements not found in gas giant models

hook.text: Super-Earths are planets between 1 and 10 Earth masses. Our solar system has none, but they're the most common type found.

buildup.text: They might be rocky like Earth or wrapped in thick hydrogen envelopes. We don't know for sure yet.

discovery.text: Kepler found that super-Earths are extremely common around other stars. Their absence here is the real mystery.

twist.text: Jupiter may have prevented super-Earths from forming in our solar system by disrupting their building materials early on.

climax.text: Some super-Earths sit in habitable zones. They're prime targets in the search for signs of life beyond Earth.

punchline.text: The galaxy's most common planet type. We don't have one. That's the puzzle.

quiz.question: What makes super-Earths unusual in the context of our solar system?
options: Our solar system has none, despite them being the most common planet type
They are the only planets known to have liquid water
They orbit farther from their stars than any planet in our system

hook.text: Mini-Neptunes are small gas-rich planets between super-Earths and Neptune in size. They have thick atmospheres over rocky cores.

buildup.text: They're typically 2 to 4 Earth radii. Kepler data shows they're one of the most common planet types in the galaxy.

discovery.text: There's a gap in the size distribution—few planets exist between 1.5 and 2 Earth radii. It's called the 'radius valley.'

twist.text: Radiation from the host star may strip atmospheres off smaller mini-Neptunes, turning them into bare super-Earths.

climax.text: Understanding this valley could reveal whether a planet keeps or loses its atmosphere—key for habitability.

punchline.text: Not quite Neptune, not quite Earth. A cosmic identity crisis in the radius valley.

quiz.question: What is the 'radius valley' in exoplanet science?
options: A gap in planet sizes where few planets between 1.5 and 2 Earth radii exist
A valley on mini-Neptunes caused by atmospheric erosion
The distance range where no exoplanets have been found

hook.text: Some exoplanets may be covered entirely by deep global oceans—water worlds with no land at all.

buildup.text: If a planet has enough water and the right temperature, oceans could be hundreds of kilometers deep.

discovery.text: K2-18b showed water vapor in its atmosphere in 2019. It may be a 'Hycean' world—a hydrogen-rich ocean planet.

twist.text: Deep enough oceans create exotic high-pressure ice at the bottom—not from cold, but from extreme pressure alone.

climax.text: JWST detected possible signs of dimethyl sulfide on K2-18b—a molecule made by life on Earth. The debate rages on.

punchline.text: Worlds of endless ocean. One might even carry a chemical hint of life.

quiz.question: What makes K2-18b a significant exoplanet discovery?
options: JWST detected possible biosignature molecules in its atmosphere
It is the closest ocean world to our solar system
It has a visible ocean surface photographed by telescopes

hook.text: Rogue planets drift through space without orbiting any star. They're alone in the dark between the stars.

buildup.text: They were likely ejected from their original systems by gravitational encounters with other planets.

discovery.text: Microlensing surveys suggest billions of rogue planets wander the Milky Way—possibly outnumbering stars.

twist.text: Some rogues could retain internal heat. A thick hydrogen atmosphere might trap enough warmth for liquid water underground.

climax.text: JWST found free-floating planet-mass objects in Orion—some in pairs, challenging formation models.

punchline.text: Billions of starless worlds drifting in darkness. Some might still be warm inside.

quiz.question: How do most rogue planets end up without a star?
options: Gravitational encounters eject them from their original planetary systems
They form independently in interstellar gas clouds without any star
Their host stars explode, leaving the planets behind

hook.text: The habitable zone is the distance from a star where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface. Not too hot, not too cold.

buildup.text: Earth sits comfortably inside the Sun's habitable zone. Venus is too close, Mars is at the outer edge.

discovery.text: Every star has its own habitable zone. Small red dwarfs have them close in; hot blue stars push them far out.

twist.text: Being in the zone doesn't guarantee habitability. Venus is nearly in it but has a runaway greenhouse making it 460°C.

climax.text: The concept guides target selection for missions like JWST and future telescopes searching for biosignatures.

punchline.text: Not too hot, not too cold. But being in the zone is just the beginning.

quiz.question: Why doesn't being in the habitable zone guarantee a planet is habitable?
options: Other factors like atmosphere and greenhouse effects determine surface conditions
The habitable zone only applies to gas giants, not rocky planets
Stars in the habitable zone eventually destroy their planets

hook.text: Red dwarfs are the most common stars in the galaxy. Their habitable zones are close in, making planets easy to find.

buildup.text: Planets in these zones orbit in days or weeks. They're tidally locked—one side always faces the star.

discovery.text: Proxima Centauri b orbits the nearest star in its habitable zone. It's only 4.2 light-years from Earth.

twist.text: Red dwarfs are violent—frequent flares could strip a planet's atmosphere and sterilize its surface with radiation.

climax.text: Whether red dwarf planets can retain atmospheres is one of the biggest open questions in exoplanet science today.

punchline.text: The nearest habitable-zone world is next door. But its star might be too angry.

quiz.question: What major challenge do planets face in red dwarf habitable zones?
options: Frequent stellar flares may strip their atmospheres and sterilize surfaces
They orbit too far from the star to receive any warmth
Red dwarfs don't emit enough light for photosynthesis

hook.text: Many habitable-zone exoplanets are tidally locked—one hemisphere always faces the star, the other is in permanent darkness.

buildup.text: The day side could be scorching hot while the night side freezes. This creates extreme weather at the boundary.

discovery.text: Climate models show that if the atmosphere is thick enough, winds can carry heat to the night side, evening things out.

twist.text: The terminator line—the boundary between day and night—might be the most habitable region on such a planet.

climax.text: JWST is studying tidally locked planets to test whether their atmospheres survive or get blasted away by stellar activity.

punchline.text: Eternal day on one side, eternal night on the other. Life might prefer the edge.

quiz.question: Where might conditions be most habitable on a tidally locked planet?
options: Along the terminator line between the permanent day and night sides
On the day side where the star provides maximum energy
On the night side where temperatures are most stable

hook.text: Gas giants can't support life, but their moons might. A habitable moon orbiting a giant planet is a real possibility.

buildup.text: In our solar system, Europa and Enceladus have subsurface oceans. Similar moons might exist around exo-gas-giants.

discovery.text: Kepler found hints of exomoon candidates, but confirming them is extremely difficult with current technology.

twist.text: A large moon orbiting a gas giant in the habitable zone could be warmed by both its star and tidal heating from the planet.

climax.text: If exomoons are common, the number of potentially habitable worlds in the galaxy could be far larger than we thought.

punchline.text: Not just planets—moons could be habitable too. The search is far wider than we imagined.

quiz.question: Why could moons of gas giants in the habitable zone be habitable?
options: They receive heat from both the star and tidal forces from the planet
Gas giants shield moons from all harmful radiation
Moons always have thick atmospheres that trap heat efficiently

hook.text: Liquid water might exist far outside the traditional habitable zone. Tidal heating and internal energy change the rules.

buildup.text: Europa orbits Jupiter far from the Sun's habitable zone, yet it has a liquid ocean heated by tidal forces.

discovery.text: Enceladus shoots geysers of water into space—proof of a subsurface ocean on a tiny moon in the outer solar system.

twist.text: If tidal heating works here, it works around exoplanets too. Habitable oceans could exist in the coldest, darkest orbits.

climax.text: The traditional zone focuses on surface water. Subsurface oceans expand the potentially habitable real estate enormously.

punchline.text: Life doesn't need sunlight to have liquid water. The zone is bigger than we thought.

quiz.question: How can liquid water exist far outside the traditional habitable zone?
options: Tidal heating from a nearby planet can warm subsurface oceans
Solar winds heat the atmosphere of distant moons
Dark matter provides gravitational energy that melts ice

hook.text: When an exoplanet transits its star, starlight filters through its atmosphere. Each gas leaves a unique fingerprint.

buildup.text: Different molecules absorb specific wavelengths. By comparing starlight during and outside transit, we read the atmosphere.

discovery.text: Sodium was the first molecule detected in an exoplanet atmosphere in 2001—on the hot Jupiter HD 209458b.

twist.text: Clouds and hazes can block the signal, producing flat, featureless spectra. Many early targets were frustratingly opaque.

climax.text: JWST's infrared power cuts through clouds much better. It's already detecting water, CO₂, and more on distant worlds.

punchline.text: Starlight through alien air. Each wavelength tells us what that world breathes.

quiz.question: How does transmission spectroscopy reveal an exoplanet's atmosphere?
options: Starlight filtered through the planet's atmosphere shows absorption lines from its gases
Telescopes photograph the atmosphere directly and analyze its color
Radio signals bounced off the planet's surface reveal atmospheric density

hook.text: Biosignatures are atmospheric gases that could indicate life. Oxygen, methane, and ozone are key targets.

buildup.text: On Earth, oxygen is overwhelmingly produced by photosynthesis. Without life, it would vanish from our atmosphere.

discovery.text: Finding oxygen and methane together would be compelling—they destroy each other without a living source to replenish them.

twist.text: Abiotic processes can also produce oxygen and methane. A single gas alone is never proof—context matters enormously.

climax.text: JWST and future missions aim to detect these gases. A credible biosignature detection would transform human understanding.

punchline.text: Oxygen plus methane together. That combination shouldn't exist without life.

quiz.question: Why would finding oxygen and methane together in an atmosphere be significant?
options: They react and destroy each other, so both need a continuous source like life to coexist
Together they form water, which is essential for life
They are only found in atmospheres of planets with magnetic fields

hook.text: Exoplanet weather makes Earth's worst storms look gentle. Some worlds have winds over 8,000 km/h and molten glass rain.

buildup.text: HD 189733b is a hot Jupiter with silicate clouds. The rain is literally molten glass blown sideways by hypersonic winds.

discovery.text: Phase curve observations track brightness changes as the planet rotates, mapping temperature differences across its surface.

twist.text: Some hot Jupiters have day-side temperatures of 4,600°C—hotter than many stars. Iron and titanium vaporize there.

climax.text: WASP-76b may have iron rain: iron vaporizes on the scorching day side and condenses into rain on the cooler night side.

punchline.text: Glass rain, iron rain, hypersonic winds. Alien weather at its most extreme.

quiz.question: What makes the weather on WASP-76b so extreme?
options: Iron vaporizes on the day side and rains down as liquid on the cooler night side
It has permanent hurricanes larger than the planet Jupiter
Its entire atmosphere freezes and thaws every orbit

hook.text: Some planets are losing their atmospheres. Intense radiation from their stars strips gas away into space.

buildup.text: Hot Neptunes close to their stars are especially vulnerable. Ultraviolet light heats the upper atmosphere until it escapes.

discovery.text: GJ 3470b is losing its atmosphere at a rate of 10 billion grams per second—it's literally evaporating.

twist.text: This explains the 'hot Neptune desert'—a gap where few Neptune-sized planets exist very close to their stars.

climax.text: Atmospheric escape may transform mini-Neptunes into bare rocky super-Earths over billions of years.

punchline.text: Stars can boil planets down to their cores. Neptune today, super-Earth tomorrow.

quiz.question: What is the 'hot Neptune desert'?
options: A gap where few Neptune-sized planets exist close to their stars, likely due to atmospheric stripping
A dry region on Neptune-like planets caused by solar radiation
An area in the galaxy where no Neptune-mass planets have been found

hook.text: JWST is transforming exoplanet atmosphere science. Its infrared instruments detect molecules invisible to earlier telescopes.

buildup.text: It has already detected water, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide in exoplanet atmospheres with stunning clarity.

discovery.text: On WASP-39b, JWST produced the most detailed exoplanet spectrum ever—revealing photochemistry driven by its star's light.

twist.text: JWST can study smaller, cooler planets too. It's targeting rocky worlds in habitable zones for the first time.

climax.text: Early results from TRAPPIST-1 planets suggest some may lack thick atmospheres—a sobering but crucial finding.

punchline.text: Infrared eyes on alien skies. Every spectrum rewrites what we know.

quiz.question: What is JWST's advantage for studying exoplanet atmospheres?
options: Its infrared instruments detect molecules invisible to earlier telescopes
It can land probes on exoplanets to sample their atmospheres
It orbits close enough to exoplanets to photograph their surfaces

hook.text: TRAPPIST-1 has seven Earth-sized planets orbiting a tiny red dwarf just 40 light-years away. Three are in the habitable zone.

buildup.text: The planets are packed incredibly tight. From one world, you could see the others as large as our Moon in the sky.

discovery.text: The system was found by a small Belgian telescope in 2016. Follow-up studies revealed all seven planets within a year.

twist.text: The planets are in a resonant chain—their orbital periods form neat ratios, suggesting they migrated inward together.

climax.text: JWST is studying each planet's atmosphere. Early results are already reshaping our understanding of red dwarf planets.

punchline.text: Seven worlds, three in the zone. Forty light-years away—our best shot at finding life.

quiz.question: What is special about the orbits of the TRAPPIST-1 planets?
options: They form a resonant chain with orbital periods in neat ratios
They all orbit in opposite directions around the star
Each planet has exactly the same orbital period

hook.text: Proxima Centauri b is the closest known exoplanet to Earth—just 4.2 light-years away, orbiting the nearest star.

buildup.text: It sits in the habitable zone and has a minimum mass of 1.2 Earths. Its year is just 11.2 days.

discovery.text: Guillem Anglada-Escudé's team found it in 2016 using the radial velocity method, analyzing years of precision data.

twist.text: Proxima Centauri is a flare star. A 2018 superflare was 10 times more powerful than any recorded solar flare.

climax.text: Whether it has an atmosphere—or if the star's flares have stripped it bare—is a major target for future observations.

punchline.text: The nearest exoplanet, potentially habitable. But its star has a violent temper.

quiz.question: What is the main concern about habitability on Proxima Centauri b?
options: The host star's frequent and powerful flares may have stripped its atmosphere
It orbits too far from its star to receive enough heat
It is too massive for liquid water to exist on its surface

hook.text: Kepler-186f was the first Earth-sized planet found in the habitable zone of another star. It was a milestone discovery.

buildup.text: It orbits a red dwarf 580 light-years away. It receives about one-third the energy Earth gets from the Sun.

discovery.text: Announced in 2014, it proved that Earth-sized planets in habitable zones actually exist. Theory became reality.

twist.text: We know almost nothing else about it. It's too far and too faint for current telescopes to study its atmosphere.

climax.text: It remains a symbol—proof of concept that worlds like ours orbit other stars. The real study awaits better instruments.

punchline.text: Earth-sized, in the zone. Proof that our kind of world exists elsewhere.

quiz.question: Why was Kepler-186f such an important discovery?
options: It was the first Earth-sized planet confirmed in a star's habitable zone
It was the first exoplanet found with oxygen in its atmosphere
It orbits the same type of star as our Sun

hook.text: HR 8799 has four giant planets that have been directly photographed—the first multi-planet system seen this way.

buildup.text: The planets are massive—5 to 10 times Jupiter's mass—and orbit far from their star, making imaging easier.

discovery.text: Christian Marois and his team captured the first images in 2008. Later they added a fourth planet closer to the star.

twist.text: The system is young—only 30 million years old. The planets still glow with the heat of their formation.

climax.text: Spectra of these planets reveal water and carbon monoxide in their atmospheres—direct chemical analysis of alien worlds.

punchline.text: Four planets, directly seen. We read their atmospheres from 130 light-years away.

quiz.question: Why is the HR 8799 system important for exoplanet science?
options: It was the first multi-planet system to be directly photographed
It contains the most Earth-like planet ever discovered
It is the closest planetary system to our Sun

hook.text: 55 Cancri e is a super-Earth orbiting so close to its star that its surface is likely an ocean of molten lava.

buildup.text: Its year is just 18 hours. Surface temperatures reach about 2,000°C—hot enough to melt most rocks.

discovery.text: It was one of the first super-Earths found, detected in 2004 orbiting a nearby Sun-like star just 41 light-years away.

twist.text: Early reports suggested it was made of diamond—carbon compressed under extreme conditions. That claim remains debated.

climax.text: JWST detected thermal emission from its surface in 2024, hinting at a possible atmosphere despite the extreme heat.

punchline.text: A year in 18 hours. Lava oceans and maybe diamonds under the crust.

quiz.question: What makes 55 Cancri e extreme even among exoplanets?
options: It orbits so close that its surface is molten lava with a year of just 18 hours
It is the largest known rocky planet at 50 Earth masses
It has the coldest surface temperature ever measured on a super-Earth

hook.text: NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will launch in the mid-2020s. It carries a coronagraph to image exoplanets directly.

buildup.text: Its wide field of view will survey millions of stars. Microlensing observations will detect thousands of new planets.

discovery.text: Roman will find planets down to Mars mass using microlensing—far smaller than any current method can detect at distance.

twist.text: Its coronagraph is a technology demonstrator. If successful, it will prove the concept for directly imaging Earth-like planets.

climax.text: Combined with JWST, Roman will give us the most complete census of exoplanets ever assembled.

punchline.text: Thousands of new planets expected. The next great leap in planet hunting.

quiz.question: What detection method will the Roman Space Telescope primarily use to find new exoplanets?
options: Gravitational microlensing to detect planets as small as Mars
The transit method by staring at a single star field for years
Radial velocity measurements of nearby Sun-like stars

hook.text: NASA's proposed Habitable Worlds Observatory aims to directly photograph Earth-like planets around Sun-like stars.

buildup.text: It would use a large UV-optical-infrared telescope with an advanced coronagraph to block starlight with extreme precision.

discovery.text: The Astro2020 Decadal Survey recommended it as the top priority flagship mission for the next generation.

twist.text: It aims to study 25 potentially habitable worlds in detail—searching for water, oxygen, and other biosignatures.

climax.text: If built, it could answer whether Earth-like planets with signs of life exist. A launch in the 2040s is the target.

punchline.text: Photograph an Earth twin. Search its air for life. The ultimate mission.

quiz.question: What is the primary goal of the Habitable Worlds Observatory?
options: To directly photograph Earth-like planets and search their atmospheres for signs of life
To land robotic probes on nearby exoplanets
To detect gravitational waves from planetary formation

hook.text: To see an exoplanet next to its star, you must block the star's blinding light. Two technologies compete for this job.

buildup.text: A coronagraph sits inside the telescope and blocks light internally. A starshade flies in space tens of thousands of km ahead.

discovery.text: Coronagraphs have improved a millionfold since the 1930s. Modern ones can suppress starlight by a factor of 10 billion.

twist.text: A starshade must maintain alignment with a distant telescope to within one meter—while both are thousands of km apart.

climax.text: Both approaches are being developed. Whichever succeeds could enable the first true photograph of an Earth-like world.

punchline.text: Block the star, see the planet. Two ideas racing to reveal alien Earths.

quiz.question: What is the main challenge of using a starshade?
options: It must maintain precise alignment with the telescope from thousands of kilometers away
It blocks all light, making it impossible to see the planet too
It can only be used once before needing replacement

hook.text: Beyond biosignatures, scientists are searching for technosignatures—evidence of alien technology in exoplanet data.

buildup.text: Industrial pollution, artificial lights on a night side, or megastructures could theoretically be detected from afar.

discovery.text: Tabby's Star dimmed erratically, sparking speculation about an alien megastructure. The cause turned out to be dust.

twist.text: NASA now funds technosignature research seriously. It's no longer fringe science but a growing field.

climax.text: If a civilization exists with technology far beyond ours, its footprint might be visible across interstellar distances.

punchline.text: Not just biology—technology leaves traces too. We're learning to look for both.

quiz.question: What are technosignatures?
options: Detectable evidence of alien technology such as industrial pollution or megastructures
Natural signals from pulsars mistaken for alien communications
Patterns in cosmic microwave background radiation from advanced civilizations

topics.title: How Many Earths Are Out There?

hook.text: Astronomers estimate that one in five Sun-like stars has a rocky planet in its habitable zone. That's billions of candidates.

buildup.text: Kepler data showed rocky planets are common. The Drake equation's biggest unknown is getting clearer.

discovery.text: Eta-Earth—the fraction of stars with Earth-like planets—is estimated at 10–20%. The galaxy may host 10 billion such worlds.

twist.text: Having a rocky planet in the zone doesn't mean it's habitable. Atmosphere, water, and magnetic fields all matter.

climax.text: Future missions will narrow the estimate. Each new planet tells us whether our kind of world is common or precious.

punchline.text: Ten billion possible Earths in our galaxy alone. The universe may be full of them.

quiz.question: What does 'eta-Earth' refer to in astronomy?
options: The estimated fraction of Sun-like stars with Earth-sized planets in habitable zones
The rate at which Earth-like planets lose their atmospheres
The distance ratio between Earth and the nearest habitable exoplanet
